long_reaction_chains.name: Long Reaction Chains
long_reaction_chains.description: Multiple reaction steps where each step's product becomes the reactant for the next

structural_patterns: Product of one step becomes reactant of next

counting_enumeration.name: Counting/Enumeration
counting_enumeration.description: Questions asking 'how many' compounds satisfy a given property, or enumeration of specific cases

deep_mechanistic_reasoning.name: Deep Mechanistic Reasoning
deep_mechanistic_reasoning.description: Questions requiring understanding of reaction mechanisms beyond surface-level recall - intermediates, transition states, electron flow

comparative_ranking.name: Comparative Ranking
comparative_ranking.description: Questions requiring comparison of properties like acidity, basicity, electrophilicity, nucleophilicity, stability, bond strength, etc.

questions.question_text: Fructose is an example of: (1) Pyranose (2) Ketohexose (3) Aldohexose (4) Heptose
questions.solution_summary: Fructose is a ketohexose - it has 6 carbons and a ketone group (C₂). It is not an aldose, pyranose refers to ring structure.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of fructose classification.

questions.question_text: The set of elements that differ in mutual relationship from those of the other sets is: (1) Li - Mg (2) B - Si (3) Be - Al (4) Li - Na
questions.solution_summary: Li-Mg, B-Si, Be-Al show diagonal relationship. Li-Na are in the same group, not diagonal. So Li-Na differs.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of diagonal relationship in periodic table.

questions.question_text: The functional groups that are responsible for the ion-exchange property of cation and anion exchange resins, respectively, are: (1) -SO₃H and -NH₂ (2) -SO₃H and -COOH (3) -NH₂ and -COOH (4) -NH₂ and -SO₃H
questions.solution_summary: Cation exchangers contain acidic groups like -SO₃H or -COOH. Anion exchangers contain basic groups like -NH₂. The correct pair is -SO₃H (cation) and -NH₂ (anion).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of ion-exchange resin functional groups.

questions.question_text: Match List-I and List-II: List-I (Ores): A. Haematite B. Bauxite C. Magnetite D. Malachite. List-II (Formulas): I. Al₂O₃·xH₂O II. Fe₂O₃ III. CuCO₃·Cu(OH)₂ IV. Fe₃O₄.
questions.solution_summary: Haematite → Fe₂O₃ (A-II); Bauxite → Al₂O₃·xH₂O (B-I); Magnetite → Fe₃O₄ (C-IV); Malachite → CuCO₃·Cu(OH)₂ (D-III).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Matching of ores with formulas - pure recall.

questions.question_text: The correct pair(s) of the ambient nucleophiles is (are): (A) AgCN/KCN (B) RCOOAg/RCOOK (C) AgNO₂/KNO₂ (D) AgI/KI
questions.solution_summary: Ambident nucleophiles have two different nucleophilic sites. AgCN/KCN (can attack through C or N) and AgNO₂/KNO₂ (can attack through N or O) are ambident nucleophiles.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identification of ambident nucleophiles.

questions.question_text: The set that represents the pair of neutral oxides of nitrogen is: (1) NO and N₂O (2) N₂O and N₂O₃ (3) N₂O and NO₂ (4) NO and NO₂
questions.solution_summary: NO and N₂O are neutral oxides of nitrogen. N₂O₃ and NO₂ are acidic oxides.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of neutral oxides of nitrogen.

questions.question_text: Match List-I with List-II: List-I (Complexes): A. [Co(NH₃)₆][Cr(CN)₆] B. [Co(NH₃)₃(NO₂)₃] C. [Cr(H₂O)₆]Cl₃ D. cis-[CrCl₂(ox)₂]³⁻. List-II (Isomerism): I. Linkage isomerism II. Solvate isomerism III. Co-ordination isomerism IV. Optical isomerism.
questions.solution_summary: [Co(NH₃)₆][Cr(CN)₆] → Co-ordination isomerism (A-III); [Co(NH₃)₃(NO₂)₃] → Linkage isomerism (B-I); [Cr(H₂O)₆]Cl₃ → Solvate isomerism (C-II); cis-[CrCl₂(ox)₂]³⁻ → Optical isomerism (D-IV).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Matching of complexes with types of isomerism.

questions.question_text: Primary, secondary and tertiary amines can be separated using: (1) Para-Toluene sulphonyl chloride (2) Chloroform and KOH (3) Benzene sulphonic acid (4) Acetyl amide
questions.solution_summary: Para-toluene sulphonyl chloride (Hinsberg's reagent) is used to separate primary, secondary and tertiary amines based on their different reactions.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of Hinsberg's test for amine separation.

questions.question_text: The common positive oxidation states for an element with atomic number 24, are: (1) +2 to +6 (2) +1 and +3 to +6 (3) +1 and +3 (4) +1 to +6
questions.solution_summary: Chromium (Z=24) shows common oxidation states from +2 to +6. +1 is very rare and unstable.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of oxidation states of chromium.

questions.question_text: Match List-I with List-II: List-I (Chemical Compound): A. Sucralose B. Glyceryl ester of stearic acid C. Sodium benzoate D. Bithionol. List-II (Use): I. Synthetic detergent II. Artificial sweetener III. Antiseptic IV. Food preservative.
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Chemistry in Everyday Life
questions.solution_summary: Sucralose → Artificial sweetener (A-II); Glyceryl ester of stearic acid → Synthetic detergent (B-I); Sodium benzoate → Food preservative (C-IV); Bithionol → Antiseptic (D-III).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Matching of compounds with their uses - pure recall.

questions.question_text: Given below are two statements: Statement-I: 2-methylbutane on oxidation with KMnO₄ gives 2-methylbutan-2-ol. Statement-II: n-alkanes can be easily oxidised to corresponding alcohol with KMnO₄.
questions.solution_summary: Statement I is true: Tertiary hydrogen in 2-methylbutane can be oxidized to alcohol with KMnO₄. Statement II is false: n-alkanes are very unreactive and are not easily oxidized.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of alkane oxidation reactivity.

questions.question_text: Nitrogen can be estimated by Kjeldahl's method for which of the following compound?
questions.solution_summary: Kjeldahl's method is not applicable to compounds containing nitrogen in nitro groups, azo groups, or nitrogen in ring (pyridine). Option 2 is the compound that can be estimated by Kjeldahl's method.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of limitations of Kjeldahl's method.

questions.question_text: Amongst the following, the linear species is: (1) NO₂ (2) Cl₂O (3) O₃ (4) N₃⁻
questions.solution_summary: N₃⁻ (azide ion) is linear (sp hybridisation, isoelectronic with CO₂). NO₂ is bent, Cl₂O is bent, O₃ is bent.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identification of linear species using VSEPR theory.

questions.question_text: C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁ + H₂O → Enzyme A → C₆H₁₂O₆ + C₆H₁₂O₆; C₆H₁₂O₆ → Enzyme B → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂. In the above reactions, the enzyme A and enzyme B respectively are:
questions.solution_summary: Sucrose hydrolysis: C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁ + H₂O → Invertase → Glucose + Fructose (Enzyme A = Invertase). Glucose fermentation: C₆H₁₂O₆ → Zymase → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂ (Enzyme B = Zymase).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of enzyme names in sucrose hydrolysis and fermentation.

questions.question_text: One of the by-products formed during the recovery of NH₃ from Solvay process is: (1) Ca(OH)₂ (2) NaHCO₃ (3) CaCl₂ (4) NH₄Cl
questions.solution_summary: In Solvay process: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂; CaO + H₂O → Ca(OH)₂; 2NH₄Cl + Ca(OH)₂ → 2NH₃ + CaCl₂ + 2H₂O. CaCl₂ is the by-product.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of Solvay process and by-products.

questions.question_text: C₇H₇N₂OCl + C₂H₅OH → N₂ + 'X' + 'Y' (A). In the above reaction, the structural formula of (A), 'X' and 'Y' respectively are:
questions.solution_summary: The reaction involves diazonium salt (C₇H₇N₂OCl) with ethanol. The products N₂, X and Y are identified based on the reaction mechanism.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of diazonium salt reactions with ethanol.

questions.question_text: For the coagulation of a negative sol, the species below, that has the highest flocculating power is: (1) SO₄²⁻ (2) Ba²⁺ (3) Na⁺ (4) PO₄³⁻
questions.solution_summary: For negative sol, cations cause coagulation. Higher charge on cation means higher flocculating power. Ba²⁺ (charge +2) > Na⁺ (+1). SO₄²⁻ and PO₄³⁻ are anions and coagulate positive sols. So Ba²⁺ is highest.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires comparison of flocculating power based on charge.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statement(s) is (are) incorrect reason for eutrophication? (A) excess usage of fertilisers (B) excess usage of detergents (C) dense plant population in water bodies (D) lack of nutrients in water bodies that prevent plant growth
questions.solution_summary: Eutrophication is caused by excess nutrients (A and B) leading to dense plant growth (C). Lack of nutrients (D) prevents plant growth and is NOT a cause of eutrophication. So D is incorrect.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of eutrophication causes.

questions.question_text: Choose the correct statement regarding the formation of carbocations A and B given: CH₃-CH₂-CH=CH₂ + HBr → CH₃-CH₂-CH₂-CH₂⁺ + Br⁻ (A) and CH₃-CH₂-CH⁺-CH₃ + Br⁻ (B). (1) Carbocation B is more stable and formed relatively at faster rate (2) Carbocation A is more stable and formed relatively at slow rate (3) Carbocation B is more stable and formed relatively at slow rate (4) Carbocation A is more stable and formed relatively at faster rate
questions.solution_summary: Carbocation B (secondary, CH₃-CH⁺-CH₃) is more stable than A (primary, CH₃-CH₂-CH₂-CH₂⁺). More stable carbocation forms faster (lower activation energy). So B is more stable and formed faster.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires comparison of carbocation stability and its effect on formation rate.

questions.question_text: During which of the following processes, does entropy decrease? (A) Freezing of water to ice at 0°C (B) Freezing of water to ice at -10°C (C) N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) → 2NH₃(g) (D) Adsorption of CO(g) on lead surface (E) Dissolution of NaCl in water
questions.solution_summary: Entropy decreases when disorder decreases: (A) Liquid → Solid (ΔS negative); (B) Liquid → Solid at lower T (ΔS negative); (C) 4 moles gas → 2 moles gas (ΔS negative); (D) Adsorption (ΔS negative); (E) NaCl dissolution (ΔS positive). So A, B, C and D show decrease.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of entropy change in various processes.

questions.question_text: A KCl solution of conductivity 0.14 S m⁻¹ shows a resistance of 4.19 Ω in a conductivity cell. If the same cell is filled with an HCl solution, the resistance drops to 1.03 Ω. The conductivity of the HCl solution is ____ × 10⁻² S m⁻¹. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of cell constant to find conductivity.

questions.question_text: On complete reaction of FeCl₃ with oxalic acid in aqueous solution containing KOH, resulted in the formation of product A. The secondary valency of Fe in the product A is ____. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
questions.solution_summary: FeCl₃ + 3KOH + 3C₂O₄²⁻ → K₃[Fe(C₂O₄)₃] (A). Secondary valency (coordination number) of Fe in [Fe(C₂O₄)₃]³⁻ is 6 (3 bidentate oxalate ligands).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of ligand denticity and coordination number.

questions.question_text: The reaction 2A + B₂ → 2AB is an elementary reaction. For a certain quantity of reactants, if the volume of the reaction vessel is reduced by a factor of 3, the rate of the reaction increases by a factor of ____. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
questions.sub_topic: Effect of volume change on rate
questions.solution_summary: Rate = k[A]²[B₂]. When volume reduced by factor 3, concentration becomes 3 times. New rate = k(3[A])²(3[B₂]) = 27k[A]²[B₂] = 27 times.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of rate law with concentration change due to volume reduction.

questions.question_text: The total number of C-C sigma bond/s in mesityl oxide (C₆H₁₀O) is ____. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
questions.solution_summary: Mesityl oxide: CH₃-CO-CH=C(CH₃)₂. C-C sigma bonds: CH₃-CO (1), CO-C (1), C=C (1 sigma), =C-C (1), CH₃-C (1). Total = 5.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires counting of C-C sigma bonds from structure.

questions.question_text: A 1 molal K₄Fe(CN)₆ solution has a degree of dissociation of 0.4. Its boiling point is equal to that of another solution which contains 18.1 weight percent of a non electrolytic solute A. The molar mass of A is ____ u. (Round off to the Nearest Integer). [Density of water = 1.0 g cm⁻³]
questions.solution_summary: K₄Fe(CN)₆ → 4K⁺ + [Fe(CN)₆]⁴⁻. i = 1 + 4α = 1 + 4×0.4 = 2.6. Effective molality = 2.6. For non-electrolyte, molality = 2.6. 18.1 wt% means 18.1 g solute in 81.9 g water. M = (18.1×1000)/(81.9×2.6) = 85.0 g/mol.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Complex colligative property calculation with van't Hoff factor.

questions.question_text: In the ground state of atomic Fe (Z=26) the spin-only magnetic moment is ____ × 10⁻¹ BM. (Round off to the Nearest Integer). [Given: √3=1.73, √2=1.41]
questions.solution_summary: Fe: [Ar]3d⁶4s². Ground state has 4 unpaired electrons. μ = √(4(4+2)) = √24 = 4.898 BM = 48.98 × 10⁻¹ BM ≈ 49 × 10⁻¹ BM.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Magnetic moment calculation from electron configuration.

questions.question_text: The number of chlorine atoms in 20 mL of chlorine gas at STP is ____ × 10²¹. (Round off to the Nearest Integer). [Assume chlorine is an ideal gas at STP, R = 0.083 L bar mol K⁻¹, N_A = 6.023×10²³]
questions.sub_topic: Gas volume to molecules at STP
questions.solution_summary: PV = nRT. At STP: 1 bar × 0.02 L = n × 0.083 × 273 → n = 8.82×10⁻⁴ mol. Number of Cl₂ molecules = 8.82×10⁻⁴ × 6.023×10²³ = 5.31×10²⁰. Number of Cl atoms = 2 × 5.31×10²⁰ = 1.06×10²¹ ≈ 1 × 10²¹.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Ideal gas equation to find number of atoms.

questions.question_text: KBr is doped with 10⁻⁵ mole percent of SrBr₂. The number of cationic vacancies in 1 g of KBr crystal is ____ × 10¹⁴. (Round off to the Nearest Integer). [Atomic Mass: K:39.1 u, Br:79.9 u, N_A=6.023×10²³]
questions.solution_summary: 1 mole KBr = 119 g. Moles in 1 g = 1/119. Sr²⁺ replaces 2 K⁺, creating 1 cation vacancy per Sr²⁺. Moles of SrBr₂ = (10⁻⁵/100) × (1/119) = 8.4×10⁻¹⁰. Vacancies = 8.4×10⁻¹⁰ × 6.023×10²³ = 5.06×10¹⁴ ≈ 5 × 10¹⁴.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Calculation of cation vacancies from doping percentage.

questions.question_text: Consider the reaction N₂O₄(g) ⇌ 2NO₂(g). The temperature at which Kc = 20.4 and Kp = 600.1 is ____ K. (Round off to the Nearest Integer). [Assume all gases are ideal and R = 0.0831 L bar K⁻¹ mol⁻¹]

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of Kp-Kc relationship.

questions.question_text: Consider the above reaction. The percentage yield of amide product is ____. (Round off to the Nearest Integer). (Given: Atomic mass: C:12.0 u, H:1.0 u, N:14.0 u, O:16.0 u, Cl:35.5 u)
questions.solution_summary: Moles of acid chloride = 0.140/140.5 = 9.96×10⁻⁴ mol. Theoretical yield of amide = 9.96×10⁻⁴ × 273 = 0.272 g. Actual yield = 0.210 g. % yield = (0.210/0.272) × 100 = 77.2% ≈ 77%.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Percentage yield calculation from stoichiometry.
